sentences.text: I'm Lou Joseph.
Herman Ludwig Joseph.
H-E-R-M-A-N L-U-D-W-I-G, and then J-O-S-E-P-H.
'Lutz' when I was growing up--
--when-- mostly during the-- mostly the, the-- all the time that I was in Germany, yeah.
February 24, 1927.
I'm 69 years old.
Berlin, Germany.
My father's name was Professor [GERMAN], Dr.
Ernst Joseph.
And my, my mother was Herta Sommerfeld, S-O-M-M-E-R-F-E-L-D.
Ernst.
E-R-N-S-T, Joseph.
Joseph, J-O-S-E-P-H.
Yeah.
[GERMAN] is a, a very rare designation for a physician who has, has acclaimed [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] extremely high professional standards.
My father was a orthopedic surgeon who had his own private hospital of 240 beds in Berlin.
And he-- that he owned with two other Jewish physicians.
He was Jewish, yes.
Yeah.
My mother was not Jewish.
She was Protestant.
Hmm.
Well, the very beginning wasn't-- was really kind of pleasant because it was just-- I mean, I was just growing up just before.
My father could continue to work as, as-- was a physician-- practicing physician and running his hospital until the-- March of 1933.
Hitler took over on January 30, 1933.
And within about three months, my father got a notification from the then Nazi German Medical Association that his license has been revoked because he was Jewish.
And that meant, of course, the clinic had to be closed.
So he, [SIGHS] he could not practice any more.
And these two other physicians also were notified the same way.
So we-- he could not do anything then.
And he was also very sick.
Then my father and my mother figured-- tried to figure out some way how we could maintain our standard of living and have a continued income.
And my mother had no experience in running a clinic.
She was a physician's wife.
She, she agreed to take over the clinic, if that was possible.
And-- as--
Yes, it was possible, as long as she was no longer married to my father.
She could not-- she could not be married to a Jewish man at the time.
But as an-- she was Aryan.
So that-- here, I'm bringing in for the first time the designation Aryan.
But that-- every German was classified after the takeover by the Nazis in 1933, whether they were Aryan, or non-Aryan, or half-Aryan.
A non-Aryan was a Jewish person.
And half-Aryan was a half-Jewish person, or a Mischling.
'Mischling' means that you were mixed race.
The Germans--
--didn't care-- the German-- Nazi government did not care about what religion-- you could have-- I could have been Catholic.
I could have been Protestant.
As long-- they went by-- strictly by the-- by the racial background.
And so that's how they made this very significant distinction between, you know, between Aryan and non-Aryan.
Yeah--
--but I was too young at the time, though, that it really-- and that it--
--could affect me.
But it certainly affected my parents.
So my mother had to agree to a divorce, which was totally pro forma.
And my father moved out of our apartment.
And he found another-- he moved to another section of Berlin.
And my mother made arrangements, prepared for taking over the clinic.
However, the 240-bed clinic was impossible.
That-- they would not-- it was-- they would not allow her to run this clinic.
What they did-- the clinic actually occupied two different buildings.
My mother was allowed to take the smaller building, which had only-- which could only accommodate 44 patient beds and gave-- give the clinic a new name.
The, the clinic that my father had owned and operated was called Bulow Sanatorium, named after the street, Bulow Street, where it was.
Now the clinic that my mother started to run was Winterfeldt Sanatorium, which was on the-- on the street-- on the street named Winterfeldt Street.
And so that was the-- that was the next step that we had to go through.
And she did.

But in the beginning, it was very, very difficult for her to, to get the-- get the clinic going because she had-- she-- the condition was spelled out that has to take-- since she is an Aryan who was married now, in the past tense, to a Jew, she's obligated to take a-- to take on, as the chief of the medical staff, a doctor who was a member of the Nazi party.
And it turned out that this man was a, a real difficult person and who completely took advantage of the situation.
It was a wonderful opportunity for him to, to, you know, to make himself rich.
He might have been just a doctor that had just a, a minor position somewhere else.
And now he suddenly was the, the big honcho and taking over the clinic.
And he had no-- he didn't have to worry about being difficult to work with because my mother had no recourse to complain to anyone.
She had to live with it.
She had to endure him.
She had to just had to keep, keep-- you know, put up with it.
Well, we had a--
Yes.
We had a governess that my mother hired because she now had to devote full time to running the clinic.
So somebody had-- we were, you know, seven-- I was seven.
My brother-- no, eight years old.
My brother was six years old.
Somebody had to, you know, to take care of us during the day, see that we get to school.
And we--
--were both--
We were both enrolled in, in, in public schools, grade schools.
These were just regular public grade schools, where you-- but the German school system is different.
I don't know how it is today.
But it was only four years of grade school.
And then it's eight years of high school, which would be-- the first four years would be, like, comparable to the junior high school here in this country.
My brother and I, yeah.
Dieter.
Yeah, he's still alive, yeah.
I visit him quite-- in Germany.
He lives in Munich, Germany, yeah.
His name spells D-I-E-T-E-R.
Yeah, Dieter.
Yeah, it's a pretty popular German name.
[DRINKS]
Not really, no.
At the-- at the time, I did not hear it.
Only, only those [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] experiences came after, after I was a little, you know, older.
I was-- that my mother, after my father-- well, first of all, my father was permitted-- he was reinstated by the German Medical Association, I think, in-- I believe a year later.
And I think all the physicians--
I think that all the physicians were reinstated with-- on the-- with the stipulation that they could only treat Jewish patients.
And they have to have a sign on their practice in blue with a Jewish star on it, which would signify that they can only-- that they are Jewish, and they can only treat Jewish patients.
So he was doing that.
So he did this from out of his apartment where he was living near-- he still had a-- he was still seeing some, some private-- he was still conducting a private practice.
Yes.
He had this opportunity.
And it's very unfortunate that he-- that he didn't do it.

He could have left that anywhere-- Jews could leave Germany, I think, up until-- not too many problems up until 1938, the night when the Crystal Night, which is now, you know, known as the official, you know, start of the Holocaust really, in a way.
Because that's when they smashed the Jewish doors, and burned synagogues, and, and also killed some of the Jewish people in Germany.
By SS-- by-- no, by the Storm Troopers.
That was not the SS.
That was the S-- the, the Schutzstaffel.
Well, he had a--
He had a completely [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] non-caring attitude about the situation because he felt he was a German first.
He had been a major in World War I in charge of a German military field hospital in Hungary.
Because, if you remember, in World War I, Germany, Austria, and Hungary were all united allies against Russia, Britain, France, and later on, against the US.
And so he-- you know, on his-- in his apartment was a-- was a picture of him in full German officer's uniform with all the decorations.
And he felt very strongly about being a German.

And when-- and I, I, I, I don't-- I, I, I didn't witness those things, those discussions that my father and my-- had with my mother, and her brother, my uncle, who was a pacifist, who had-- who had been a contentious objector to the draft and World War I.
Arno, yeah-- my-- his, his name was Arno, A-R-N-O Sommerfeld.
That's my mother's name-- maiden.
And he was an actor and a playwright.
And he was a pacifist.
And it goes without saying that he was a anti-Nazi.
I mean, and he was-- my mother was always telling me that my father was standing in front of the television set--
I'm sorry.
Not television.
I mean the--
Yeah, in front of the radio when Hitler was giving one of his five-hour rambling talks, where he was saying nothing.
But he was lambasting and attacking the Jews and, and holding them responsible for Germany's miseries and depression.
And my father, according to what my mother told me, was standing in front of the radio and was holding his sides and laughing himself senseless, and saying, the man is an idiot.
He says, he's never going to do that, you know.
It's impossible.
He's, he's kidding, you know.
And-- while my uncle was saying to him, Ernst, take your family, transfer all your money, and go to Switzerland.
That's the nearest country where you're going to be OK, you know.
Many others have done it-- did it, of course, at the time.
And not only going to Switzerland, but they went to England, to the United States, Canada, and Australia, you know.
So that-- he would not listen to it.
My mother wanted to leave, too.
Yeah, she was afraid because she could see already.
I mean, a, a, a government that is-- authorities that are capable of making them be divorced because they're Jewish, what would be their next step, you know?
That would be much more drastic than what they have-- what they're doing now-- what they-- at the time.
So this was all-- this was all leading up to a, a very, you know, frightening course of events.
But my father did not believe, believe that this could, you know, that it could happen.

We, we had to-- you know, the-- coming back to the clinic, my mother really had a very hard time managing with-- managing this sanatorium, with-- working together with this German-- with that Nazi doctor.
Because he used every opportunity he had to try to, you know, make it difficult for her.
And so it, it was not-- it was really not a good experience.
We were-- we had a, a very hard time financially, economically, because we, we couldn't take, you know, take much money out of the-- from-- out of the income from the clinic.
We had to put it back.
Because he made it so impossible that we, you know, could have a-- take more out of it, more-- yes.
Yes, we had finances-- we, we had problems with economical-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] with the economical situation.
I believe that we had to-- at one time, we had to give up our apartment.
We just-- you know, the rent was too high.
And we had to convert a part of a clinic floor into an apartment for us.
And I remember coming back from school, you know, and going through the emergency room [LAUGHS] to get up to our-- to get up to our apartment.
And it's very difficult.
Then, ironically, the, the, the situation got better after my mother hired a woman who was chief of the nursing staff, who was a perfect match for this Nazi doctor, who hated the Nazis, too.
She was not a party member.
And she became an ally of my-- all of my, my mother.
And she, she was forceful in dealing with this.
And so our situation improved somewhat, you know.
We had-- we could find another apartment that-- more to our life-- you know, standard of living.
And we didn't have to stay in the-- in the-- no longer in, in the clinic.
So-- that was-- that was the period of one-- the end of one period there.
I'd like to mention one more thing, though.
There are-- there was always an undercurrent of anti-Semitism.
Maybe not just an undercurrent, maybe it was more of an-- a very obvious symptom in, in Germany.
Before the-- long before Hitler took over, it might have not been during the Kaiser's-- I mean, I mean, you know, up to the period of World War I.
But I think it developed because a lot of the terrible times after-- the hard times-- economic-- hard economic times after World War I, in that period of what they called the Weimar Republic.
I, I, I believe a lot of Germans felt that the Jewish people had something that they, you know, they were doing.
Because they were doing better.
Most of the Jews in Germany did well, you know, and all-- very prominent in all walks of life.
So that they would-- that that might have been accountable for or contributing to the misery of some of the economic miseries of some of the German people.
So there was always a current of anti-Semitism in, in Germany.
And I think it was very well-- very well expressed in one incident that my mother always told me.
When my father and his, his brother, who was the chairman of-- was a, a urologist and was chairman of the urology department of the University of Berlin Medical School.
What was his name?
His name was Eugen Joseph.
And when they were-- they-- my father wouldn't drive.
I mean, he had a driver's license, but he always drove in a chauffeured-driven limousine in those days.
And this was an incident apparently that was around 1929, 1930, '31.
And they were taking a ride out in the vicinity of Germany, around Berlin and just-- you know, on a, a pleasure ride.
And the driver got lost on the-- in-- on the highway.
And he stopped.
And he-- there were some farmers working in the fields nearby.
It must have been in the summertime.
And he motioned one of the farmers to come over.
And the farmer came.
And he, he looked-- and he was going to ask him, you know, for directions.
And the farmer didn't answer.
And he looked into the back of the limo.
And he saw my parents, and my, my father's brother and his wife.
And he just says, Jews, and walked away.

So certainly there-- that was-- even before Hitler came to power, there was a-- Hitler found very willing subjects when he-- you know, that opinion of people that were already, you know, formed about the Jewish persecution.

Now, my, my wife's-- my uncle, my father's brother, was a-- had a very tragic ending, which-- he was so despondent when he got his letter that he could no longer-- he was-- he had a large private practice.
And when he, you know, got the notification that he was-- his license was withdrawn, he just could never mentally recover from it.
So what he did is he got so despondent that he-- and despaired that he committed suicide on Christmas Day, I believe, 1933.
And that devastated, of course, his, his wife and the two-- my two cousins, their two daughters.
And they went-- they immediately-- they made-- they acted what my father probably should have done.
They went and-- went and immigrated to Switzerland immediately after that.
And they [LAUGHS] were able to, probably, to transfer most of their money to the Swiss banks.
And they lived there.
So that was this part of the, you know, the history in-- pre-war history.
I did not really feel any discrimination in, in grade school because--
--like I said--
In public school, no, no, no, no, no.
No.
Yeah, there was-- you know, I didn't feel any discrimination because [SIGHS] there were-- people were too young.
And the teachers were not-- you were just-- you know, that was before the severe prosecution of the Jews really started.
So that--
Well, it's about from 1933 to 1938, '39, just almost up to the beginning of the war, not quite.
I think I started high school in 1938.
That was after Kristallnacht.
And that's when, of course, I had to-- I was already registered as a half-Jew, a half-Aryan person.
Well, you-- that was automatically.
I mean, you had to-- you had to fill out every time-- well, as you entered school, there was a questionnaire that you had, an application form to school, which listed that question.
And that's where you had to declare yourself.
Yeah, right, Aryan or non, yes or no.
And that's-- and then, then if you say no, you're either a Jew or, in my case, a half-Jew.
And you have to spell that out.
They didn't call you, really, a half-Jew.
They called you 'Mischling.'
'Mischling,' which means, really, if you roughly translate it, I would guess it would mean 'of mixed race, mixed origin.'
[DRINKS]
Yes, there were.
There were at least two categories.
But there might have even-- there might have been even further subsets.
The first category was the category that I was in.
And that was people who had one parent was Jewish and one parent was not Jewish.
The next category was where only the grandfather, one grandfather, was Jewish.
So they were considered quarter of-- quarters-- a quarter Jewish.
I mean, nobody used that term.
But that-- they were still, like, in a certain sense, a Mischling.
And I don't know how far-- how, how much to-- if they-- you know, extended to even more previous generations.
Two, two categories were definitely established.

And there were-- later on, it was determined that during the Nuremberg War Crime Trials, that the Final Solution of the Nazi-- of the Jewish problem was that, after the extermination of all the people that were, you know, full Jewish, that the next extermination would be the-- of all the people that were half-Jewish.
And then possibly the-- that another step would have been the, the people that were a quarter Jewish.
I mean, but they were--
No.
Not that I was aware of.
But it was-- there was not that much of a stigma in the beginning.
The-- it became a stigma after I went to high school, you know, when I enrolled, and especially after the war had started.
Well, [SIGHS] it, it turned out that I was the only person-- I mean, I, I, I, I attended school until 1942.

In June of 1942, at the end of that school year, a new regulation, a new directive came out from the Nazi Education Department that anybody that is half-Jewish can no longer be-- can no longer attend a, you know, high school, a secondary school.
And, in fact, you couldn't attend any school.
You couldn't-- you couldn't even go grade school.
So the question arises-- what, what could you do?
How could you choose a profession or trade for you-- for the rest of your life, I mean, on the-- when you need an education?
And the answer is, you can't.
You can't even be an unskilled laborer.
That is exactly the-- that was exactly what I was told when I was-- when I was called into the dean's office in 1942, in June.
And telling me-- and actually, the dean was very nice.
He was not-- he probably was a Nazi member, I can't remember.
But he was a human person.
And he, he-- I really felt that he honestly regretted that he had to tell me that I could no longer attend the school.
But he couldn't-- he couldn't do anything about the regulations.
Yeah, when I was in high school-- and that was already after the, you know, after the war was-- 1940, and '41.
And unfortunately, Germany, at that time, was celebrating one military victory after the other.
The, the German U-boats were sinking, you know, Allied ships in the Atlantic, supplying, you know, England with [GERMAN] and, and military hardware from the United States-- constantly.
I mean, the-- you couldn't listen to the radio.
Every, every half an hour, there was a special announcement interrupting the regular program.
Another U-boats had sank another 10, 10 freighters.
And so the-- everybody was charged up in Germany.
They were really in a-- in an overwhelming mood of victory.
I mean, it was so-- it seemed like that nothing is going to stop Germany from, from winning this war.
That was actually before the US really entered.
The US didn't enter until, you know--
--after--
--December.
Well, I remember it, as, again, as another thing that the-- another optimistic event for Germany.
They said, now, Japan is in the war with us.
And that's the Axis-- Germany-- Nazi Germany, Fascist Italy, and now Japan.
And that's going to mean that those three nations are going to win this war.
And so--
Yeah.
[DRINKS]
Well, it was--
This was certainly an unpleasant time for me.
I mean, I wanted to learn and benefit as much as I could from, from, from an education.
But it was almost made impossible.
I mean, take-- everybody in my-- this was an all-- I went to a high school that was a public school.
But it wasn't-- and no coeds.
It was all males.
It was Hohenzollern Oberschule, H-O-H-E-N-Z-O-L-L-E-R-N Oberschule, which means gymnasium, you know, high school.
And everyone in this class was a-- was a member of the Hitler Youth.
You had to join the Hitler Youth movement.
It was a junior group, like the, the little Boy Scouts, where we-- at six--
Cub Scouts.
Cub Scouts, yeah.
And now-- and then you have to-- and then you move up to regular scouts.
So they were-- so we were what, 14, 15 years olds-- 13, 14, 15.
They-- these were the regular-- these were members of the regular Hitler Youth movement.
They were all wearing brown shirts with an-- a swastika armband and then summer shorts, brown shorts, and, of course, in the winter, long pants.
So every Wednesday was the regular meeting day for-- was their regular meeting day.
And that was an impossible situation for me because [LAUGHS] I was the only one.
I, I stuck out like a sore thumb.
Everybody sitting there in uniforms-- 30, 30 boys-- and I am in, in regular clothes.
So, you know, that was unpleasant.
When I get up, my-- the English teacher asked me to give a-- read my, my work-- my homework.
He-- they would call-- they would say, shut up, Jew.
Sit down.
We don't want to hear you.
So it was not an unpleasant-- you know, a highly unpleasant situation.
You're welcome.
